coredata.dc:title: HFCM-LSTM: A novel hybrid framework for state-of-health estimation of lithium-ion battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Mingyu
Bao, Zhengyi
Zhu, Chunxiang
Jiang, Jiahao
He, Zhiwei
Dong, Zhekang
Song, Yining
coredata.dc:description: Accurate estimating the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion battery plays a significant role in the safe operation of electric vehicles. With the development of deep learning, neural network-based methods have attracted much attention from researchers. While most of the existing SOH estimation methods are built by a single network, failing to sufficiently extract data information, and thus leading to the limited accuracy and generality (i.e., such a single network makes it difficult to estimate the SOH of battery, with different types and working conditions). Towards this issue, a novel hybrid network, called HFCM (Hierarchical Feature Coupled Module)-LSTM (Long–short-term memory), is designed to fully extract the original data information, making it more accurate to estimate the SOH of battery, with different types and working conditions. Specifically, the proposed HFCM-LSTM mainly consists of two components, HCFM and LSTM. The HCFM is proposed to comprehensively extract the original data feature information from the original samples. On the other hand, following the HFCM, a LSTM module is employed to model time series information. Based on this HFCM-LSTM network, the data obtained directly from the battery are fed into the model as input, enabling an end-to-end SOH estimation of the battery. A series of experiments are conducted on both NASA dataset and Oxford dataset, the experimental results demonstrate that the proposed SOH estimation algorithm outperforms several existing state-of-the-art methods, in terms of accuracy and generality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
State-of-health
Long–short-term memory (LSTM)
Hybrid network

Estimation performance of the proposed HFCM-LSTM network structure
Experiment on NASA dataset and Oxford dataset

In [N, M], N denotes the sequence length and M denotes the number of channels.

head.ce:title: HFCM-LSTM: A novel hybrid framework for state-of-health estimation of lithium-ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronics and Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronics and Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronics and Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018,China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhejiang Provincial Key Lab of Equipment Electronics, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhejiang Provincial Key Lab of Equipment Electronics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhejiang Provincial Key Lab of Equipment Electronics, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Training Center, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Training Center, China Jiliang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Training Center, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 310018,China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhejiang Leapmotor Technology Co., Ltd, 310053, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhejiang Leapmotor Technology Co., Ltd, 310053, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate estimating the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion battery plays a significant role in the safe operation of electric vehicles. With the development of deep learning, neural network-based methods have attracted much attention from researchers. While most of the existing SOH estimation methods are built by a single network, failing to sufficiently extract data information, and thus leading to the limited accuracy and generality (i.e., such a single network makes it difficult to estimate the SOH of battery, with different types and working conditions). Towards this issue, a novel hybrid network, called HFCM (Hierarchical Feature Coupled Module)-LSTM (Long–short-term memory), is designed to fully extract the original data information, making it more accurate to estimate the SOH of battery, with different types and working conditions. Specifically, the proposed HFCM-LSTM mainly consists of two components, HCFM and LSTM. The HCFM is proposed to comprehensively extract the original data feature information from the original samples. On the other hand, following the HFCM, a LSTM module is employed to model time series information. Based on this HFCM-LSTM network, the data obtained directly from the battery are fed into the model as input, enabling an end-to-end SOH estimation of the battery. A series of experiments are conducted on both NASA dataset and Oxford dataset, the experimental results demonstrate that the proposed SOH estimation algorithm outperforms several existing state-of-the-art methods, in terms of accuracy and generality.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets in this manuscript can be found in https://ti.arc.nasa.gov/tech/dash/groups/pcoe/prognostic-data-repository/ and https://howey.eng.ox.ac.uk/data-and-code/.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing demand for energy conservation and the reduction of emissions, new energy vehicles have gradually replaced traditional fossil fuel vehicles. As one of the important components of electric vehicle (EV), lithium-ion batteries are widely used in the assembly of EV’s battery packs due to their high energy density and long cycle life (Wang et al., 2021a). To ensure the safety and durability of EV operation, it is critical to accurately estimate the health status of lithium-ion batteries. State of health (SOH) is a quantitative indicator of battery health, which is defined as the ratio of the current capacity to the rated capacity of the battery and is often used to indicate the relationship between the operating state and the service life of the battery. Because of the importance of SOH to the operation of battery manage systems (BMS), researchers have conducted a lot of studies on SOH estimation.

Existing SOH estimation methods are roughly classified into model-based methods and data-driven methods. Model-based methods are mainly developed based on electrochemical models (Chen et al., 2021a), equivalent circuit models (ECMs) (Vichard et al., 2021), empirical models (Tran et al., 2021), which focus on the battery degradation process by analyzing the internal physical and chemical reaction mechanisms and external electrical characteristics. Electrolyte decomposition, formation of solid electrolyte interphase layers between solid electrolytes, and dissolution of materials are the chemical mechanisms of battery capacity loss. Based on these mechanisms, researchers have proposed many different electrochemical models to estimate the battery SOH. Han et al. (2015) proposed a pseudo-two-dimensional (SP2D) model to achieve online simulation in real vehicle. In Zheng et al. (2016), the authors proposed trinal proportional–integral observers based on the electrochemical model to simultaneously estimate the SOC, SOH and resistance of lithium-ion batteries. Li et al. (2018) proposed a degradation model based on a single-particle model to accurately capture the cell state. However, the electrochemical model-based approaches have to deal with the complex partial differential equations, which increases the computation burden on the BMS and is therefore not suitable for application in EV BMS. The widely used ECMs include first-order RC model, second-order RC model, Thevenin model, Rint model and models based on the composite improvement of the above circuits. This type of method first extracts features from the charging/discharging voltages, and then uses Gaussian regression algorithms (Lin et al., 2022), least squares method (Wang et al., 2017), etc., to establish the connection between parameters and SOH. Yu et al. (2019) used a recursive least squares algorithm for Thevenin ECM parameter identification, which enable the estimated capacity to converge to near the rated capacity value despite inaccurate initial parameter values. To achieve accurate estimation of SOC and SOH, Fang et al. (2019) established a battery ECM and used Forgetting Factor Recursive Least Squares to realize online identification of model parameters. However, the method based on the ECM is highly influenced by the operating conditions and is prone to unreliable modeling performance, making it difficult to be applied to the SOH estimation task with a combination of dynamic and static conditions. The empirical model-based approach is to model the relationship among battery overcharge and over-discharge, charge-to-discharge ratio, battery life decay, etc. This method is tested at different decay factors and the SOH mathematical expressions are fitted with different decay factors as independent variables. On the basis of incremental capacity analysis, Tang et al. (2018) proposed a novel method for estimating SOH in real time using a constant current curve during charging. Based on electrochemical-impedance spectroscopy test data, Guha and Patra (2017) developed an empirical model for internal resistance growth. However, the empirical model-based approach is highly dependent on the quality of the data set samples, and thus it is hard to accurately reflect the battery decay process.

Such model-based methods are highly dependent on the accuracy of the established model, but paradoxically, due to the limitations of experimental conditions, there is no accurate model that can accurately describe the aging behavior of the battery. As an alternative solution, a data-driven method does not rely on a specific model. Instead, it directly learns the internal dynamic characteristics of the battery through a large amount of charge and discharge data. A nonlinear relationship between SOH and original data such as voltage, current, and temperature is established. For a long time, approaches such as particle filtering, support vector regression (SVR) (Li et al., 2022), support vector machine (SVM) (Feng et al., 2019), relevance vector machine (RVM) (Chen et al., 2021b), etc., have been employed to monitor the degradation behavior of the battery capacity. Li et al. (2020) proposed a data-driven prediction technique, SVR for establishing a battery degradation model, which estimates battery capacity by partial incremental capacity curves. Shateri et al. (2021) used two techniques, nonlinear cure fitting and SVM classification, to generate SOH values according to the identified parameters. Wang et al. (2021b) proposed a SOH prediction method based on multi-kernel RVM and whale optimization algorithm. The above methods have higher accuracy and robustness, but still have some limitations. For example, SVR-based methods need to pre-process a large amount of data, so their computational complexity increases greatly; the kernel function of SVM-based methods must satisfy the Mercer condition, so it is easy to fall into local optimality and difficult to determine the loss function; RVM-based methods tend to have less stable prediction results due to their high sparsity.

With the maturity of deep learning technology, neural network-based battery estimation methods have received more and more attention in recent years. To overcome the limitations of traditional data-driven methods, NN-based methods approximate high-dimensional nonlinear functions directly from the original data, providing high prediction accuracy while solving complex problems. Among them, recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are widely used for SOH prediction in batteries due to their superiority in processing time series. Chaoui and Ibe-Ekeocha (2017) proposed an RNN-based approach for the joint estimation of SOC and SOH. Che et al. (2020) established an improved dynamic recurrent neural network (DDRN) with the ability of dynamic mapping, which is more suitable for estimating the batteries’ state with non-linear and dynamic behaviors. However, RNNs suffer from gradient explosion and gradient disappearance, so it is difficult to deal with the problem of long-term network dependence, which is well solved by the emergence of long–short-term memory (LSTM) (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997). Li et al. (2021) developed a data-driven capacity estimation model based on LSTM for cells under real-world working conditions, which has guaranteed robustness even when handling input noise. Lin et al. (2021), He et al. (2021) and Heinrich and Pruckner (2022) used LSTM to capture the long-term tendency of battery degradation, which can be adapted to real-world customer usage. Compared to RNN, LSTM can improve SOH estimation accuracy by capturing long-term dependencies through specific gate structures. However, estimation methods based on single neural networks often have certain limitations of their own. In detail, firstly, data-driven methods rely on a large amount of original data, and insufficient extraction of data by single neural network model-based methods will result in inaccurate estimation results; secondly, for a trained single estimation neural network, it is often only applicable to a specific battery cell or a specific operating mode, and the estimation effect of the framework will be limited when the battery is replaced. Therefore, this type of method is more influenced by external factors and has weaker robustness.

Motivated by the above analyses, in this paper, a new hybrid network, called HFCM-LSTM, is built to accomplish the battery SOH estimation task in a more accurate and general manner. The proposed HFCM-LSTM network consists of two components, HFCM and LSTM, the HFCM is proposed to extract time series features, and the LSTM is proposed to model time series information. Considering the environmental diversification in the actual operating state of EV, battery data with different working conditions are used for experiments, which have proved that the proposed network is able to complete the task of online end-to-end SOH estimation well, and can be applied to different types of batteries with different operating conditions, with good universality and robustness. The main contributions are summarized as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Aiming at the problems of inaccurate estimation results caused by insufficient feature extraction, a novel feature extraction module HFCM with two parallel streams is proposed in this paper.
A new hybrid framework with cascaded configuration (i.e., HFCM-LSTM) is designed to realize the high-precision end-to-end battery SOH estimation under different working conditions.
To verify the proposed scheme, two public lithium-ion battery datasets are used in this work. The experimental results demonstrate the accuracy and generality of the proposed hybrid network.

ce:section.ce:para: The rest of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 describes the proposed HFCM module and the HFCM-LSTM based SOH estimation method; the dataset and experiments are presented in Section 3; Finally, Section 4 concludes the paper.

In this paper, we propose a HFCM to extract data features with different semantic information from original data and use LSTM to model time series information. More importantly, we cascade the two modules together to form an end-to-end SOH estimation network. This network can fully extract and process the original battery information to achieve a more accurate estimation.

In this section, a new fusion module HFCM is proposed to solve the problem of inadequate time series feature extraction. The overall structure of the HFCM is shown in Fig. 1, it is clear that this module contains two streams at different depths, i.e., a deep feature extraction stream and a shallow feature extraction stream.

The deep feature extraction stream consists of three dilated blocks cascaded with a SE block, where each dilated block consists of a dilated convolutional layer (Yu and Koltun, 2015) and a ReLU activation function. Compared with ordinary convolution, dilated convolution increases the receptive field without losing information, so that each convolution contains a larger range of information under the same computational condition and is better at capturing multi-scale contextual information. The size of the convolutional kernel of the dilated convolutional layer is calculated as follows:
(1)
k
∗
=
k − 1
d − 1
+ k
where
k
∗
is the kernel size of the dilated convolutional layer, k is the kernel size of the ordinary convolution layer, d is the dilation rate. Dilated convolution enables the network to have a larger receptive field with a smaller number of layers, but it also introduces new problems. The inputs to the dilated convolution are spaced, so not all inputs are involved in the next step of the computation, especially when the dilation rates are the same, and there will be a discontinuity of convolution centroids on the overall features. Therefore, dilated convolutions with different dilation rates, namely 1, 2, 6, are adopted to alleviate this problem in this paper. The schematic diagram of dilated convolution used in this paper is shown in Fig. 2. The receptive field of the dilated convolution is calculated as follows:
(2)
r
n
=
r
n − 1
+
k
∗
− 1
∗
∏
i = 1
n − 1
S
i
where
r
n
is the receptive field of the dilated convolution,
r
n − 1
is the receptive field of the previous layer of the dilated convolution,
k
∗
is the kernel size of the dilated convolutional layer,
S
i
is the step size of the i th convolution layer or pooling layer, n is the number of layers of convolutional layers.

When estimating the battery SOH, each feature channel has a different impact on the final estimation result, so this paper introduces the attention mechanism to better identify this difference. After three dilated blocks, the data is input to the next SE block. SE block is composed of two parts, squeeze and excitation (Hu et al., 2018). Squeeze gets the current global compressed feature volume by global average pooling on feature channels, and excitation gets the weight of each channel by two fully connected layers, which can effectively fuse the information between channels and help information interaction. Finally, after the weights are assigned to each channel, the input of the next layer can be obtained.

As shown in Fig. 3, the input of the SE block is one-dimensional data. The size of the input feature map can be calculated by
X
c
= C × S × 1
, where C and S are channel number and sequence length of input feature map respectively. Squeeze performs the global average pooling for features of size
C × S × 1
on the input, getting the features of size
C × 1 × 1
. The global average pooling operator used in this paper is as follows:
(3)
F
s q
X
c
=
1
S
∑
i = 1
S
X
c
i

The attention weight of the C-th channel in the SE block is as follows:
(4)
W
c
= σ
W
1
η
W
0
X
c
where
W
0
and
W
1
represent the weight parameters of the two fully connected layers, respectively. η represents the activation function after the first fully connected layer, σ represents the activation function after the second fully connected layer. In addition, ReLU and hard sigmoid units are used as the first and second activation function, respectively, which are calculated as follows:
(5)
η
X
c
= max
0,
X
c
(6)
σ
X
c
=
ReLU 6
X
c
+ 3
6
where ReLU6 is min(6, max(0, x)). In fact, the sigmoid function is generally used as second activation function, but different from this, this paper uses the hard sigmoid function. According to (Louizos et al., 2017), the hard sigmoid function is much more computationally efficient than the sigmoid function. Their comparison diagram is shown in Fig. 4.

The proposed network uses the relevant sequence information to learn the weight distribution through the SE block, and then assigns the learned weights to the original series data for output estimation. SE block has more nonlinearities than the normal fully connected layer, which can better fit the correlation between channels and reduce the number of parameters and computational effort to some extent. The deep feature extraction stream runs as follows:
(7)
F
D
=
S
E
f
D 3
f
D 2
f
D 1
X
input
where
X
input
is the output of the previous layer network,
f
D 1
is the feature extraction operator of the first dilated block,
f
D 2
is the feature extraction operator of the second dilated block,
f
D 3
is the feature extraction operator of the third dilated block,
S
E
is the feature extraction operator of the SE block.

The shallow feature extraction stream consists of a one- dimensional convolutional neural network. This stream designed in this paper has two meanings. Firstly, the extracted data can be guaranteed to have the same feature dimension before feature fusion through this stream. Secondly, this stream retains the original data information while extracting features. By fusing the output of the two extraction streams, the problem of inaccurate estimation results caused by insufficient battery data amount and insufficient extraction can be solved to a certain extent. The shallow feature extraction stream runs as follows:
(8)
F
S
X
input
=
f
1
X
input
where
X
i n p u t
is the output of the upper layer network,
f
1
is the feature extraction operator of the 1D-CNN.

Finally, the outputs of the deep feature extraction stream and the shallow feature extraction stream are fed into the next layer of the network by adaptive fusion of the outputs. The designed HFCM runs as follows:
(9)
H
X
input
=
F
D
X
input
+
F
S
X
input
where
F
D
is the operator of the deep feature extraction stream,
F
S
is the operator of the shallow feature extraction stream;
X
i n p u t
obtained from the previous layer network is the input of this fusion module.

LSTM is a variant of RNN, which adds a special gating mechanism to address the shortcomings of RNN in handling one term memory. Fig. 5 shows the internal structure of a LSTM unit. Actually, this three-inputs-two-outputs unit uses three types of gates: input gate, forget gate, and output gate to hold and control information. Specifically, the update formulas for the LSTM unit at time t are as follows:
(10)
f
t
= σ
W
f x
x
t
+
W
f h
h
t − 1
+
b
f
(11)

= σ

W

t − 1
+
b

(12)

= t a n h

(13)

(14)

(15)

where
f
t
is the forget gate,
i
t
is the input gate,
c

is the cell state candidate,
c
t
is the cell state,
o
t
is the output gate,
h
t
is the hidden layer state value;
x
t
and
h
t − 1
represent the input at moment t and the output at moment t-1 of the LSTM unit, respectively; W and b represent the weight and bias, respectively; σ and tanh represent the sigmoid activation function and tanh activation function, respectively, which are defined as follows:
(16)
σ
x
=
1

e
− x
(17)
t a n h
x

e

− x

Combining the above equations, the theory of LSTM holding and controlling information is as follows. First, the forget gate combines the previous hidden layer output
h
t − 1
with the current input
x
t
to decide which old information to discard by a sigmoid activation function, where value range of the sigmoid function is (0,1), when its value is close to 0, the layer discards this information; when the value is close to 1, the layer keeps this information. Then, the output gate, combined with tanh activation function, determine the state of the hidden layer at this moment, which is chosen from output
h
t − 1
of the previous moment, the input
x
t
of this moment, and the cell state
c
t
.

The HFCM is used to extract data features with different semantic information from input data, while the LSTM is used to model time series information. To make full use of these two networks, this paper cascades the two and proposes a hybrid network named HFCM-LSTM to complete the SOH estimation. The HFCM-LSTM hybrid network proposed in this paper can solve the problem of inaccurate results caused by insufficient data extraction to a certain extent on the basis of completing the end-to-end SOH estimation task, and is suitable for different types of single batteries under different working conditions, with good accuracy and universality. Fig. 6 shows the overall network framework based on the proposed HFCM-LSTM.

The estimation network is composed by four parts. First of all, the original data samples are injected into the 1D-CNN module of the first layer for initial data processing, which includes a 1D-CNN layer, ReLU activation function and a max-pooling layer. Then, through two cascaded HFCM modules, the data features are extracted and further input to the two cascaded LSTM modules. Finally, the estimated capacity can be achieved via two fully-connected dense layers. The parameter settings of the estimation network proposed in this paper are shown in Table 1.

Notably, the original charging data (voltage, current, temperature) has been converted to a fixed size
x
t
∈
R
N × M
for subsequent processing, where N = 4000, M = 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SOH estimation algorithm based on HFCM-LSTM network
ce:section.ce:para: The specific algorithm description of lithium-ion battery SOH estimation using the proposed network model can be divided into four steps, as shown in Fig. 7.
Step 1: Data initialization, the original discharge data are resampled using the isometric sampling method and transformed into a 4000 × 1 vector.

To eliminate the influence of the magnitude among different metrics, the original data are preprocessed using the min–max normalization method. By linearly varying the original data in this way, the final output is mapped to between [0,1], which can speed up the convergence of the network model and improve the network accuracy. The normalized formula is as follows:
(18)
X
∗
=
X
initial
−
X
m i n
X
m a x
−
X
m i n
where
X
∗
is the data after normalized pre-processing,
X
i n i t i a l
is the original data from the NASA dataset or the Oxford dataset,
X
m a x
is the maximum value in the original data sample,
X
m i n
is the minimum value in the original data sample. Then, the data samples are divided into a test set and a training set according to a certain ratio.

Step 2: Network parameter setting and optimization, set the number of input layer nodes, hidden layer nodes, and output layer nodes for each layer of the HFCM-LSTM network; determine the hyperparameters of the HFCM-LSTM (mainly refers to the batch size, epoch, and learning rate) to ensure the accuracy of the network training. In order to accelerate the network convergence process, an adaptive learning rate method (Al-Kababji et al., 2022) is applied in this paper, so that the estimation results can be obtained quickly and accurately. For the two hyperparameters of batch size and epoch, multiple experiments are conducted by the control variable method to obtain their optimal values. The initial values of hyperparameters are set as batch size = 32 and epoch = 100.

To explore the effect of two hyperparameters (batch size and epoch) on this network, this paper first keeps the batch size constant and change its epoch to obtain the loss of the network model. Then, keep the epoch value constant and change its batch size to obtain the loss of this network model. Finally, all the results are shown together in Fig. 8.

From Fig. 8, it is found that a larger batch size is easy to produce a high loss, which may cause large errors; secondly, when the epoch reaches 150, the network is close to convergence. At this time, a larger epoch for training will cause the training process too long and the loss will basically not be further reduced. Therefore, the hyperparameters used in this paper are set as batch size = 8 and epoch = 150.

Step 3: Model training, use the training data to train the proposed network. The Adam optimizer is used to optimize the loss gradient and then update the network weights. The trained HFCM-LSTM network model is saved for subsequent testing.

Step 4: Estimation, use the well-trained HFCM-LSTM network to estimate the SOH on the test data. Two loss functions, mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE) (Willmott and Matsuura, 2005), are used as evaluation criteria to evaluate the accuracy of the proposed network. The mathematical expression are as follows:
(19a)
M A E =
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
| y −
y
⌢
|
(19b)
R M S E =
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
y −
y
⌢
2
where y and
y
⌢
are the true and estimated values of the battery charging curve, respectively, and n is the total number of battery data samples.

The first dataset is Randomized Battery Usage dataset (hereafter referred to as the NASA dataset) (Saha and Goebel, 2007) collected by the NASA’s Ames Research Center. The battery type used in this dataset is LG Chem 18650 lithium-ion battery, which is capable of operating at a voltage range of 3.2–4.2 V and a rated capacity of 2.1 Ah. Ames Research Center tested and sampled 28 identical types of Lithium-ion batteries, which were divided into seven groups according to the test conditions. The final sampling yields about 50,000 data points, of which about 950 constant current–constant voltage (CC–CV) full-charge curves can be used for Lithium-ion battery SOH online estimation studies. In this experiment, we use B0007, B0028, B0032 and B0036 as the test set, and the other 9 battery cells as the training set. Most of these batteries have different working conditions. By dividing the test set and training set in this way, the effectiveness of the proposed network in different environments can be verified.

Another dataset is Oxford Battery Degradation Dataset 1 (hereafter referred to as the Oxford dataset) (Christoph, 2017) collected by the Battery Intelligence Lab at the University of Oxford. This dataset was tested for long-term battery aging using Kokam batteries, a model with a rated capacity of 0.74 Ah. The test system conducts charge/discharge experiments on 8 test Lithium-ion batteries at a constant temperature of 40 °C. Each experiment is conducted at a constant current of 2C (1.48 A) and repeatedly cycled for the purpose of accelerating aging. In this experiment, we use the Cell 4 and Cell 8 battery degradation test data as the test set and the remaining test data as the training set. Notably, the ratio of test set to training set samples is 1: 3. A comparison of these two battery datasets is shown in Table 2.

Generally, to ensure the safety and reliability of the battery system, the moment when the battery capacity is reduced to 70%–80% of the rated capacity is generally defined as the end-of-life (EOL) of the battery. The relevant description document of the NASA dataset states that the EOL is reached when the capacity of the battery is reduced to 70% (1.4 Ah) of the rated capacity (2 Ah). The decay curves of partial battery capacity in the NASA dataset and Oxford dataset are shown in Fig. 9(a) and (b), respectively.

The experimental environment used in this paper is: CPU Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-10400F CPU @ 2.90 GHz 2.90 GHz, GPU NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 Ti, video card memory 8 GB, Cuda version 11.0, using the deep learning framework TensorFlow-GPU 2.5.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation performance of the proposed HFCM-LSTM network structure
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the superiority of the proposed HFCM module in this paper, we evaluate it comparatively on the NASA dataset. Five typical competitors, i.e., the Dilated CNN-LSTM (Fotiadou et al., 2021), Deep CNN-LSTM (Jeong and Lim, 2021), Regional CNN-LSTM (Wang et al., 2019a), Convolutional-LSTM (Wang et al., 2019b) and EA-LSTM (Li et al., 2019) are selected as the compared methods. For a fair comparison, we perform the experiments by only replacing the HFCM module with the feature extraction modules introduced in these compared methods. The results are shown in Fig. 10(a), (b), (c) and (d). The left figures represent the SOH estimation results and the right figures represent the model errors in percent, a relative metric result, which can be mathematically expressed as follows:
(20)
Error =
|
SOH
T
−
SOH
E
|
SOH
T
× 100 %
where SOH
T
and SOH
E
are the true and estimated values of the SOH, respectively. As shown in these four pictures, all the models can estimate the corresponding SOHs well for B0007. While in terms of both B0032 and B0036, only our method HFCM-LSTM can still maintain an estimation error less than 4%. Besides, it can be seen that the prediction accuracy of the HFCM-LSTM network is generally higher than that of the compared networks. This is due to the fact that the HFCM-LSTM can fully extract the original series information, providing more powerful features for estimation, which other compared methods ignore some important information to different extent.

To verify the high accuracy of the proposed HFCM-LSTM framework, seven competitors are selected, including the single network models CNN (Bohlender et al., 2021), RNN (Chen et al., 2021c), the current commonly used hybrid network models CNN-RNN (Zhang et al., 2019), CNN-GRU (Dairi et al., 2021), CNN-LSTM (Bao et al., 2020), and the variants of our network, i.e., HFCM-RNN and HFCM-GRU. The results are shown in Fig. 11. Although Fig. 11(a) and (b) show that the errors of all methods are relatively small, the bright blue curve (HFCM-LSTM) achieves the smallest estimation error among the eight methods. For Fig. 11(c) and (d), the compared methods can accurately estimate the downward trend of SOH, but there is still a certain deviation from the real value. Note that, the prediction error of HFCM-LSTM for these two batteries can maintain within 4%. The advantage of our method over the compared methods can be seen more clearly in the enlarged plots. First, the estimation accuracy of the hybrid network model is generally higher than that of the single networks, which is contributed to the inability of the single network model in terms of feature extraction and series information modeling. Then, compared with other hybrid networks, the proposed HFCM-series networks can obtain the highest accuracy, especially on B0032 and B0036, and the HFCM-LSTM has the lowest error.

Furthermore, two loss functions MAE and RMSE are used as evaluation criteria, representing the degree of deviation of the estimated value from the actual value. The results are shown in Fig. 12, where Fig. 12(a) and (b) represent the MAE and RMSE of the six networks of Fig. 10, respectively. Fig. 12(c) and (d) represent the MAE and RMSE of the eight networks of Fig. 11, respectively. By calculating the results from Fig. 12, the overall MAE and RMSE of SOH estimation results are 3.40%, 2.96%, 3.48%, 3.18%, 2.36%, 1.04% and 5.46%, 5.04%, 6.14%, 5.36%, 4.18%, 1.76% for the Dilated CNN-LSTM, Deep CNN-LSTM, Regional CNN-LSTM, Convolutional-LSTM, EA-LSTM, HFCM-LSTM. Besides, for the CNN, RNN, CNN-RNN, CNN-GRU, CNN-LSTM, HFCM-RNN, HFCM-GRU, HFCM-LSTM, the overall MAE and RMSE of SOH estimation results are 3.88%, 2.39%, 3.93%, 3.47%, 3.48%, 2.59%, 2.59%, 1.04% and 6.36%, 3.78%, 6.18%, 5.6%, 6.14%, 4.44%, 4.31%, 1.76%. The results show that HFCM-LSTM has the lowest MAE and RMSE when the SOH estimation is performed for the four batteries. In particular, HFCM-LSTM achieves the great superiority for B0032 and B0036. This further verifies the effectiveness of the proposed network structure for SOH estimation.

In this section, we provide the computational times of all the different neural network models listed in Section 3.3, in form of the training time and test time for each dataset. As described in Section 3.2, all the experiments are conducted on a workstation with Intel Core i5-10400F 2.90 GHz CPU, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 Ti (8 GB on-board memory) GPU and 16 GB RAM. The 13 neural network models are implemented in Python 3.7 in combination with TensorFlow-GPU 2.5.0. Table 3 summarizes the average computational times over the 5 runs with 150 epochs per run.

As shown in Table 3, compared to the methods with different feature extraction neural networks to replace HFCM, the training time required by our proposed HFCM-LSTM is in the middle of the range. This is due to that HFCM has the more complex structure and training the HFCM module is time consuming. Meanwhile, due to the parallel structure of the HFCM, a significant amount of time consumption is saved to some extent. In addition, on the one hand, the RNN-based models take the longest time to train, over 2000 s on the NASA datasets, and over 30,000 s for the single RNN model in particular. On the other hand, the RNN variant GRU, LSTM have the significantly shorter training time, which can be kept within 300 s. This is contributed to that in the RNN implementation, the computation of the output states does not start until the full computation has been completed, which slows down the sequence processing considerably. For GRU and LSTM, GRU training is slightly faster than LSTM because GRU is a simplified version of LSTM, discarding the contextual state information processing part of LSTM. Besides, the test times of all models are presented to further analyze the energy consumption. The results prove that these neural network models can be implemented online and further integrated into battery management systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment on NASA dataset and Oxford dataset
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the superiority of the proposed network in SOH estimation task, a series of ablation experiments are conducted on NASA dataset and Oxford dataset. Considering that the HFCM module has three components, four further experiments are performed, i.e., the network model without SE block (without-SE), the network model with the dilated convolution in HCFM module transformed into ordinary convolution (without-DCNN), the network without the shallow feature extraction stream(without-fusion), the completed HFCM-LSTM. Furthermore, to ensure the accuracy of the results, the number of epoch, batch size, learning rate and other hyperparameters are kept consistent in the ablation experiments.

NASA: For NASA data set, this paper selects battery test data of B0007, B0028, B0032 and B0036 as test samples, and the other 9 battery cells as the training samples. Fig. 13 shows the SOH estimation results and errors on batteries B0007, B0028, B0032 and B0036, respectively. These four networks can effectively estimate battery SOH. Among them, it can be clearly seen that the estimation result of the proposed network of the orange curve (orange dot) is more accurate and the estimation error is smaller. The results of the other three networks are obviously offset from the actual value, especially for B0032. The method of without-fusion represented by purple curve (purple dot) has the largest estimation error, which may be due to insufficient extraction of original data samples. MAE and RMSE calculated by Eq. 19 are provided in Table 3. Specifically, the overall MAEs of the proposed network, without-SE, without-DCNN and without-fusion methods are 1.08%, 2.42%, 2.25% and 2.73%, respectively. The overall RMSEs are 1.80%, 3.93%, 3.58% and 4.47% respectively. The results indicate that in the NASA dataset, the degree of influence of these three methods on SOH estimation results, in descending order, is: without-fusion, without-SE, without-DCNN, and further prove the accuracy of the proposed network.

Oxford: Compared with NASA dataset, the battery data in Oxford dataset is relatively simple, and the charging and discharging conditions of each battery are consistent. In this paper, the test data of Cell 4 and Cell 8 batteries are selected as test samples, and the other test data are used as training samples. As can be seen from Fig. 14, the four methods all have high accuracy in this dataset, and the SOH estimation error can be basically maintained within 1%. Among them, it can be clearly seen that the estimation result of the proposed network of the orange curve (orange dot) is more accurate and the estimation error is smaller. MAE and RMSE calculated by Eq. 19 are provided in Table 4. Specifically, the overall MAEs of the proposed network, without-SE, without-DCNN and without-fusion are 0.038%, 0.085%, 0.055% and 0.081% respectively. Overall RMSE are 0.072%, 0.156%, 0.100% and 0.166%, respectively. The results indicate that in the Oxford dataset, the estimation results of methods without-SE and without-fusion are relatively poor. It may be due to the fact that without-SE does not sufficiently extract the data features and without-SE does not differentiate the information with different importance. Comparing the results with those of the NASA dataset, it is clear that the Oxford dataset has a smaller error and better accuracy. The main reason may be that the Oxford dataset acquires data samples under constant operating conditions, while the NASA data considers the influence of high temperature, low temperature, and unsteady operating conditions (see Table 5).

This paper mainly investigates the battery SOH estimation via deep learning method. Specially, a novel hybrid network model with two cascaded modules (i.e., the HFCM module and LSTM network) is proposed, where the HFCM module is used to solve the problem of inadequate extraction of time series features in battery SOH estimation, and LSTM network is used to capture the time series information well. The entire scheme solves the problem of poor estimation accuracy caused by the traditional model due to insufficient data extraction. Through the validation on NASA dataset and Oxford dataset, RMSE of the proposed network can be kept within 2.3%, indicating the effectiveness of this method. Furthermore, since the batteries used in NASA dataset and Oxford dataset have different types and working conditions, the excellent performance of HFCM-LSTM proves its generality.

Mingyu Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Zhengyi Bao: Software, Writing – original draft. Chunxiang Zhu: Investigation. Jiahao Jiang: Software, Validation. Zhiwei He: Writing – review & editing. Zhekang Dong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yining Song: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was jointly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62227802), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2020YFB1710600), the Key Research and Development Program of Zhejiang Province, China (No. 2021C01111) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang, China (No. GK229909299001-404).
